9. Identical Particles

9.1. Need for Symmetrization

Definition 11. Two particles are said to be identical if no experiment can distinguish them. Identical particles share intrinsic properties such as mass, charge, spin, and magnetic moments.

Examples: all electrons are identical, and all protons are identical.

For two identical particles, the classical Hamiltonian should be invariant under exchanging labels:

(119)

Exchange Degeneracy Problem

Suppose we have two identical spin one-half particles, where one particle is spin-up and the other is spin-down. Consider describing the system as or . If these two states are equivalent choices, then any superposition should be an equivalent choice. However, have different projections along the state , which is an unacceptabl ephysical ambiguity.

More formally, the exchange degeneracy problem is the ambiguity we face in selecting a representative for the physical state in

(120)

  • is symmetric if for all .

    • Such states form a subspace .

  • is antisymmetric if for all .

    • Such states form a subspace .

  1. and are Hermitian.

  2. For any permutation ,

For a counter example of the second identity, let . It belongs in neither nor .

Symmetrization Postulate

In a system with identical particles, the states that are physically realized are not arbitrary states in but rather are totally symmetric ( identical bosons) are totally antisymmetric ( identical fermions). That is, the states must belong to or .

This postulate solves the exchange degeneracy problem by the following claim:

Proposition 19 (Uniqueness). Let be an arbitrary state, and let be the subspace spanned by the orbit of acting on . Up to a multiplicative constant, contains at most a single state in and at most a single state in . That is, any state uniquely corresponds to a boson and fermion if they exist.
Proof

Suppose is symmetric. Write as a sum over the orbit of . Use the fact that it remains constant under /, and use Lemma 3, and show that all such states are scalar multiples of and .

□

Thus by uniqueaness the bosonic state is and the fermion state is . It may be the case that , meaning that there is no bosonic state. Some useful conditions are the following:

Proposition 20 (No Boson). If satisfies for some permutation, then .
Proof

Multiply both sides by and use the fact that .

□

Proposition 21 (No Fermion aka Pauli Exclusion Principle). If two or more of the states in appearing in are the same, then the state will fail to exist in . This means that two or more identical fermions cannot be found in the same (eigen)state (wrt some complete basis).
Proof

There exists a transposition that leaves invariant. Then , implying . No state exists.

□

Remarks

  1. Bosons and fermions have strikingly different statistical behavior.
  2. Quantum field theory plus special relativity can be used to prove the spin-statistics theorem, which shows that bosons and fermions have integer and half-integer spin, respectively.
  3. Composite particles made are also either bosons or fermions. For example, consider two hydrogen atoms, with wavefunction . Since protons and electrons are fermions, exchanging them both implies the hydrogen atom is a boson!
  4. “The postulate is in fact a description of the most general statistics of particles that live in three spatial dimensions. A closer look into the physics requires thinking of permutation-induced exchanges as physical processes in which the particles actually move in some trajectories to realize the permutations. It turns out on close examination that in three spatial dimensions there are no new possibilities, just bosons or fermions. But when space is two-dimensional, there are new possibilities. Transpositions of particles need not produce only sign factors on the wave function; general phases are in principle possible. Particles with such behavior are called anyons. We will discuss such phases at the end of this section.”

9.2. Hamiltonian and Observables

We must ensure the time evolution induced by the Hamitonian for a state in or in guarantees the state remains in or in at all times.

Let be constructed in terms of arbitrary sets of opertaors acting on the various particles, e.g adding and composing operators in the form . Then

(126)

Definition 13. A Hermitian operator is said to be a completely symmetric observable if is invariant under permutations, i.e.

(127)

From Equation 126 this is equivalent to commuting with all permutation operators.

For a system of identical particles, the Hamiltonian must be a completely symmetric observable, and thus the expectation value of all permutation operators, as well as and , are conserved. and will commute with generated by , so eigenstates of and are conserved, i.e. states will remain symmetric/antisymmetric.

A simple way of creating a completely symmetric Hamiltonian is to add single-particle Hamiltonians, assuming they don’t interact.

9.3. Building Symmetrized States and Probabilities

We can canonical construct an antisymmetric state from using the Slater determinant. For example, for a system of three fermions, we have

(128)

This works in general due to the antisymmetric properties of the determinant. In particular, the Pauli exclusion principle emerges from the fact that if two rows are the same then the determinant equals zero. For bosons, we use the matrix permanent (rtaher than determinant), is defined similarly but ignores sign of permutations.

We’ll worry about normalization later. What about wavefunctions? Let

(129)

so that

(130)

Proposition 22. If are orthonormal states, then the proper normalization is .
Proof

Obvious.

□

Now let’s discuss the interpretation of the wavefunction for a system of identical particles.

We interpret

(131)

as the probability of finding one particle at , …, and one at . Note that combinatorics is at play here. If , then the factor of drops to unity.

It is not appropriate to treat this like the wavefunction of a distinguishable system and ask what the probability of finding particle 1 at and particle 2 at is.

Example 7 (Hong-Ou-Mandel two-photon experiment, 1987). Two identical photons are simultaneously incident on the top and bottom ports of a beam splitter, with two detectors on the other side. If the photons are identical (same polarization state and phase) and arrive at the beam splitter simultaneously. Both photons reach the top detector or bottom detector with equal probability.

9.4. Particles with two sets of DOF

Suppose we have a particle with both spatial and spin degrees of freedom, so that its wavefunction comes from a product space :

(132)

Proposition 24. For a two particle system, bosons are either both symmetric or antisymmetric in their spatial and spin wavefunctions. For fermions we require one and one:

(133)

Example 8 (Two electron system). Consider wavefunctions of a 2-electron system of the form

(134)

In this separable form we can invoke Proposition 24 to get that the possible fermionic states are an antisymmetric spatial state times the singlet state or a symmetric spatial state times a triplet state:

(135)

Assuming the electrons don’t interact, we get

(136)

where depends on the overlap .

Returning to our probabilistic interpretation, we have that the probability to find both particles at is , yielding

(137)

This means the probability is enhanced relative to when the particles are distinguishable and the wavefunction is symmetric, while electrons avoid each othe when the spatial wavefunction is antsymmetric. More generally, this happens when and are near each other.

Question. Why don’t we have to symmetrize electrons across the universe?

It turns out, that if they’re wavefunctions don’t overlap, then the probability we compute is the same as if we treated the particles as distinguishable.

9.5. Occupation Numbers

Many different basis states in lead to the same symmetric/antisymmetric states when applying or . The solution is to define occupation numbers to distinguish states in that are linearly independent after applying or .

Definition 14. Let be an orthonormal basis of so that standard basis states in take the form

(138)

For each standard basis state , assign it to a set of occupation numbers where is the nmber of times appears in . Since we are describing states of particles, the list of nonzero occupation numbers is finite (must sum to ).

For bosons, each occupation number may be any natural number. For fermions, each occupation number must be zero or one.

Example 9 (2 particles in 3 state system). We have 6 possible occupation numbers out of the 9 basis states. All 6 can be bosons, be only 3 can be fermions by Pauli exclusion.

States in and are uniquely characterized by occupation numbers, as applying a permutation operator on a standard basis state does not affect the occupation number. Write

(139)

so that

(140)

If there are a finite number of energy levels , the dimensions of each space are, by combinatorics:

(141)

If , we cannot obtain any fermionic states. For example, we cannot bind more than two electrons to a delta function potential.

If using Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics (each particle is distinguishable), there are possible states.

Example 10 (Multielectron atom). What are the basis states of a multielectron atom, assuming no electron interaction?

Electrons are fermions, so a basis state of an atom is obtained by having act on a tensor basis state. Each electron has four quantum numbers: .
